head_to_toe_sequence.description: Observe appearance, hygiene, distress, and ABCs from doorway.
tips: Note work of breathing before approaching

head_to_toe_sequence.description: Temperature, pulse, respirations, BP, SpO₂, pain score.
tips: Orthostatics if fall risk or diuretics

head_to_toe_sequence.description: Head, eyes, ears, nose, throat systematic exam.
Trachea midline, thyroid, lymph nodes, JVD, carotid bruits.

tips: Note SpO₂ with exertion if ordered

head_to_toe_sequence.description: Apical pulse, heart sounds, peripheral perfusion.
tips: Apical pulse 1 full minute if irregular

head_to_toe_sequence.description: Inspect, auscultate bowel sounds, palpate quadrants.
LOC, cranial nerves, motor/sensory, reflexes as indicated.
Integument, pressure injury risk, mental status, safety screening.

body_systems.name: Neurological
body_systems.overview: Systematic neurological assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
assessment_steps: Mental status and level of consciousness
abnormal_findings: Unsteady gait, inability to move extremity
red_flags: Full neuro assessment; stroke protocol if focal deficit
nursing_implications: Document objective neurological findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

body_systems.name: Cardiovascular
body_systems.overview: Systematic cardiovascular assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
assessment_steps: Blood pressure both arms if indicated
red_flags: ECG if symptomatic; continuous monitoring if ordered
nursing_implications: Document objective cardiovascular findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

body_systems.name: Respiratory
body_systems.overview: Systematic respiratory assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
abnormal_findings: Productive cough with colored sputum, hemoptysis
nursing_implications: Document objective respiratory findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

body_systems.name: Gastrointestinal
body_systems.overview: Systematic gastrointestinal assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
red_flags: NPO if surgical concern; notify provider
nursing_implications: Document objective gastrointestinal findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

body_systems.name: Genitourinary
body_systems.overview: Systematic genitourinary assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
nursing_implications: Document objective genitourinary findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

body_systems.name: Musculoskeletal
body_systems.overview: Systematic musculoskeletal assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
abnormal_findings: Unable to bear weight after fall
nursing_implications: Document objective musculoskeletal findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

body_systems.name: Integumentary (Skin)
body_systems.overview: Systematic integumentary (skin) assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
nursing_implications: Document objective integumentary (skin) findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

body_systems.name: HEENT
body_systems.overview: Systematic heent assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
nursing_implications: Document objective heent findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

body_systems.name: Psychosocial
body_systems.overview: Systematic psychosocial assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
nursing_implications: Document objective psychosocial findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

body_systems.name: Endocrine & Immune
body_systems.overview: Systematic endocrine & immune assessment integrates inspection, palpation, auscultation, and patient-reported data.
red_flags: Vitals; TSH per order; notify provider
nursing_implications: Document objective endocrine & immune findings in flowsheet.
Compare to baseline and report changes.

red_flags_master.finding: Sudden unilateral weakness and slurred speech
red_flags_master.action: Activate stroke protocol; note last known well; notify provider; NPO until swallow eval

red_flags_master.finding: GCS decrease ≥2 points
red_flags_master.action: Neuro checks q15min; notify provider; prepare for imaging

red_flags_master.finding: Chest pain with diaphoresis and ST elevation on monitor
red_flags_master.action: 12-lead ECG; aspirin per protocol; notify provider; continuous monitoring

red_flags_master.finding: SBP <90 mmHg with altered mental status
red_flags_master.action: IV access; fluids per order; notify provider; assess perfusion

red_flags_master.finding: SpO₂ <90% with increased work of breathing
red_flags_master.action: Airway assessment; O₂; high Fowler's; notify provider

red_flags_master.finding: Stridor at rest
red_flags_master.action: Airway equipment to bedside; notify provider/rapid response

red_flags_master.finding: Silent chest in asthma with exhaustion
red_flags_master.action: Rapid response; continuous bronchodilator per order; prepare for escalation

red_flags_master.finding: Rigid abdomen with rebound tenderness
red_flags_master.action: NPO; IV access; notify surgeon/provider; vitals q15min

red_flags_master.finding: Hematemesis or melena with hypotension
red_flags_master.action: IV access; fluids; type and crossmatch per order; notify provider

red_flags_master.finding: Oliguria <30 mL/hr in adult with hypotension
red_flags_master.action: Assess volume status; notify provider; strict I&O

red_flags_master.finding: Temperature >38.3°C (101°F) in neutropenic patient
red_flags_master.action: Blood cultures per order; broad-spectrum antibiotics per protocol; notify provider

red_flags_master.finding: Active suicidal ideation with plan and means
red_flags_master.action: 1:1 observation; remove means; notify provider/charge nurse; document quotes

red_flags_master.finding: New onset confusion with fever and stiff neck
red_flags_master.action: Sepsis/meningitis workup; notify provider; prepare for LP/CT per order

red_flags_master.finding: Exophthalmos, fever, tachycardia in hyperthyroid patient
red_flags_master.action: Notify provider — thyroid storm concern; cooling, beta-blocker per order

red_flags_master.finding: Epigastric pain radiating to back, rigid abdomen (pancreatitis concern)
red_flags_master.action: NPO; IV fluids; pain management per order; notify provider

red_flags_master.finding: COPD patient SpO₂ 98% on high-flow O₂ with somnolence
red_flags_master.action: Titrate O₂ to target SpO₂ 88–92%; ABG per order; notify provider

red_flags_master.finding: Orthostatic SBP drop ≥20 mmHg with fall
red_flags_master.action: Fall precautions; fluids per order; medication review

red_flags_master.finding: Unilateral calf swelling, warmth, Homan sign unreliable
red_flags_master.action: Do not massage; notify provider; anticoagulation/imaging per order

red_flags_master.finding: Purpura + fever in immunocompromised patient
red_flags_master.action: Blood cultures; notify provider; sepsis protocol

red_flags_master.finding: Infant <28 days with rectal temp ≥38°C (100.4°F)
red_flags_master.action: Full sepsis workup per protocol; notify provider; NPO for procedures

vital_signs.pediatric_note: Pediatric norms vary by age — always use age-specific charts.
vital_signs.orthostatic_criteria: SBP decrease ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg within 3 min of standing with symptoms.

pain_assessment.nursing_priority: Assess respiratory rate and sedation before additional opioids.

skills.title: Orthostatic Vital Signs
steps: Criteria: SBP drop ≥20 or DBP ≥10 with symptoms
skills.clinical_why: Detects volume depletion and medication effects.

skills.title: Lung Auscultation
skills.clinical_why: Crackles/wheezes guide oxygen and bronchodilator therapy.

skills.title: Abdominal Assessment
skills.clinical_why: Auscultate before palpate to avoid altering bowel sounds.

skills.title: Focused Neurological Check
skills.clinical_why: Serial checks detect deterioration in stroke/TBI.

skills.title: FAST Stroke Screening
steps: Inpatient: notify provider/stroke team per protocol
skills.clinical_why: Time is brain — thrombolysis windows are limited.

skills.title: Pain Assessment (PQRST)
skills.clinical_why: Pain is subjective — believe the patient.

skills.title: Skin & Pressure Injury Inspection
skills.clinical_why: Prevention is easier than treatment of Stage 3–4 injuries.

skills.title: Suicide Risk Screening
skills.clinical_why: Asking does not increase risk — it enables safety planning.

skills.title: Pediatric Vital Signs
steps: Count RR on infant 1 full minute
skills.clinical_why: Pediatric decompensation is rapid — assess work of breathing.

skills.title: SBAR Handoff Communication
skills.clinical_why: Structured communication reduces errors during transitions.

interview_techniques.example: Tell me what brought you in today.
Is the pain worse at night?
You sound frustrated about the wait.
When you say dizzy, do you mean the room spins?
So your pain started 2 days ago after lifting...

interview_techniques.purpose: Allows patient time to process and continue.

special_populations.name: Older Adults
assessment_modifications: Include caregiver in teaching when appropriate

special_populations.name: Pregnant Patients
considerations: Supine hypotension after 20 weeks — left lateral tilt
assessment_modifications: Include fundal height and FHR when appropriate

special_populations.name: Limited English Proficiency
considerations: Professional interpreter — not family for clinical consent

soap_exercises.title: Dyspnea with Crackles

findings.subjective: Patient reports worsening shortness of breath x2 days, worse lying flat.
findings.objective: RR 28, SpO₂ 89% RA, bilateral crackles bases, BP 158/92, HR 104.
findings.assessment: Impaired gas exchange r/t pulmonary edema secondary to fluid overload.
findings.plan: High Fowler's, O₂ 2L NC titrate, daily weight, I&O, notify provider, reassess SpO₂ q1h.

model_soap.subjective: Patient reports worsening shortness of breath x2 days, worse lying flat.
model_soap.objective: RR 28, SpO₂ 89% RA, bilateral crackles bases, BP 158/92, HR 104.
model_soap.assessment: Impaired gas exchange r/t pulmonary edema secondary to fluid overload.
model_soap.plan: High Fowler's, O₂ 2L NC titrate, daily weight, I&O, notify provider, reassess SpO₂ q1h.

documentation_tips: Separate subjective quotes from objective measurements
Link assessment to NANDA-I style diagnosis
Plan includes independent nursing actions and provider notification

soap_exercises.title: Post-op Pain

findings.subjective: Patient rates incision pain 7/10, sharp, worse with movement.
findings.objective: Abdominal incision clean/dry/intact, mild guarding, BP 118/76, HR 88.
findings.plan: Analgesic per order, reposition, splint incision, reassess pain 30 min post med.

model_soap.subjective: Patient rates incision pain 7/10, sharp, worse with movement.
model_soap.objective: Abdominal incision clean/dry/intact, mild guarding, BP 118/76, HR 88.
model_soap.plan: Analgesic per order, reposition, splint incision, reassess pain 30 min post med.

documentation_tips: Separate subjective quotes from objective measurements
Link assessment to NANDA-I style diagnosis
Plan includes independent nursing actions and provider notification

soap_exercises.title: Confusion in Older Adult

findings.subjective: Family states patient more confused since last night.
findings.objective: Oriented ×1 person only, temp 38.4°C, WBC elevated per chart.
findings.plan: Urinalysis and CXR per order, fall precautions, orient frequently, notify provider.

model_soap.subjective: Family states patient more confused since last night.

Link assessment to NANDA-I style diagnosis

soap_exercises.title: Hypoglycemia

findings.subjective: Patient reports shakiness and sweating before lunch.
findings.objective: Fingerstick 54 mg/dL, diaphoretic, alert but tremulous.
findings.plan: 15 g fast carb, recheck 15 min, notify provider if <70 persists, educate on s/s.

model_soap.objective: Fingerstick 54 mg/dL, diaphoretic, alert but tremulous.
model_soap.plan: 15 g fast carb, recheck 15 min, notify provider if <70 persists, educate on s/s.

documentation_tips: Separate subjective quotes from objective measurements
Link assessment to NANDA-I style diagnosis
Plan includes independent nursing actions and provider notification

soap_exercises.title: Chest Pain

findings.subjective: Patient describes substernal pressure 8/10 started 30 min ago.
findings.objective: Diaphoretic, BP 148/90, HR 96, 12-lead ST depression V4–V6 per monitor.
findings.plan: Continuous monitoring, aspirin/nitro per protocol, IV access, notify provider, NPO.

model_soap.subjective: Patient describes substernal pressure 8/10 started 30 min ago.

soap_exercises.title: Fall with Head Strike

findings.subjective: Patient fell getting out of bed, hit head on floor.
findings.objective: 2 cm scalp laceration, GCS 14 (confused), pupils equal reactive.
findings.assessment: Risk for injury r/t fall; altered LOC r/t head trauma.
findings.plan: Neuro checks q15min, CT head per trauma protocol, wound care, fall precautions.

model_soap.subjective: Patient fell getting out of bed, hit head on floor.
model_soap.objective: 2 cm scalp laceration, GCS 14 (confused), pupils equal reactive.
model_soap.assessment: Risk for injury r/t fall; altered LOC r/t head trauma.
model_soap.plan: Neuro checks q15min, CT head per trauma protocol, wound care, fall precautions.

documentation_tips: Separate subjective quotes from objective measurements
Link assessment to NANDA-I style diagnosis
Plan includes independent nursing actions and provider notification

findings.objective: Temp 37.9°C, suprapubic tenderness, urine cloudy and odorous.
findings.plan: Urine culture, antibiotics per order, fluids, pain management, hygiene teaching.

findings.plan: Therapeutic communication, explain steps, anxiolytic per order, NPO verified.

findings.objective: Wound erythema 2 cm, purulent drainage, temp 38.1°C.
findings.assessment: Risk for infection r/t surgical site.
findings.plan: Wound culture, antibiotics per order, dressing change per protocol, notify surgeon.

findings.objective: BP 198/112, HR 82, neuro intact, lungs clear.
findings.assessment: Ineffective health maintenance r/t uncontrolled hypertension.
findings.plan: Recheck BP both arms, antihypertensive per order, neuro checks, notify provider, education.

sbar_exercises.title: Rapid Response — Hypoxemia
sbar_exercises.situation: Mr. Lee, room 412, SpO₂ dropped to 84% on 2L NC.
sbar_exercises.background: Admitted 2 days ago for pneumonia, history COPD.
sbar_exercises.assessment: RR 32, accessory muscle use, diminished bases, anxious.
sbar_exercises.recommendation: Request provider evaluation, increase O₂ per protocol, possible transfer to higher acuity.

sbar_exercises.title: Handoff — New Confusion
sbar_exercises.situation: Mrs. Brown became acutely confused this shift.
sbar_exercises.background: 88 y/o, hip repair yesterday, opioid analgesia.
sbar_exercises.assessment: Oriented ×1, new compared to morning, pupils equal, vitals stable.
sbar_exercises.recommendation: Suggest pain regimen review, UA, neuro checks q4h, fall precautions.

assess_next_scenarios.title: New Tracheostomy
assess_next_scenarios.scenario: You note thick secretions and decreased breath sounds right lung.
assess_next_scenarios.explanation: Airway patency first — suction when secretions impair ventilation.
assess_next_scenarios.clinical_why: NCLEX prioritization: unstable before stable, acute before chronic.

assess_next_scenarios.title: Chemotherapy Infusion
assess_next_scenarios.scenario: Patient develops flushing, wheezing, BP 86/50 during infusion.
options: Stop infusion, notify provider, epinephrine per anaphylaxis protocol
assess_next_scenarios.explanation: Anaphylaxis — stop infusion and treat immediately.
assess_next_scenarios.clinical_why: NCLEX prioritization: unstable before stable, acute before chronic.

assess_next_scenarios.title: Diabetic Patient NPO
options: Notify provider and verify insulin/IV fluid orders
assess_next_scenarios.explanation: Perioperative glucose management requires provider orders.
assess_next_scenarios.clinical_why: NCLEX prioritization: unstable before stable, acute before chronic.

assess_next_scenarios.title: Post-op Day 1
assess_next_scenarios.scenario: Patient has not voided 10 hours, bladder scan 450 mL.
assess_next_scenarios.explanation: Retention requires intervention to prevent AKI.
assess_next_scenarios.clinical_why: NCLEX prioritization: unstable before stable, acute before chronic.

assess_next_scenarios.title: Chest Tube
assess_next_scenarios.scenario: Sudden increase in bright red drainage 200 mL/hr.
options: Notify provider immediately, vitals, monitor output
assess_next_scenarios.explanation: Hemorrhage concern — notify provider, never clamp without order.
assess_next_scenarios.clinical_why: NCLEX prioritization: unstable before stable, acute before chronic.

assess_next_scenarios.title: Clinical Scenario 6
assess_next_scenarios.scenario: Patient develops acute shortness of breath and SpO₂ 88%.
options: Assess airway and apply O₂ per protocol
assess_next_scenarios.explanation: ABCs first — respiratory compromise takes priority.
assess_next_scenarios.clinical_why: NCLEX prioritization: unstable before stable, acute before chronic.

flashcards.front: Neurological — Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal, briskly reactive. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal: Unequal or non-reactive pupils, new anisocoria.
flashcards.abnormal_action: Full neuro check; stroke protocol if focal deficit.
flashcards.clinical_why: Pupil changes may signal ICP or stroke.

flashcards.front: Cardiovascular — Cap refill 4 sec, cool pale toes, weak pedal pulses. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.normal: Cap refill <2 sec; strong equal pulses.
flashcards.abnormal: Delayed refill, weak pulses — perfusion concern.
flashcards.abnormal_action: Vitals, IV access per order; do NOT Trendelenburg for CHF.

flashcards.front: Respiratory — Crackles at bases, SpO₂ 89%, RR 28. Normal or abnormal?
Neurological — GCS 15, oriented ×4, speech clear. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Full neuro assessment; stroke protocol if focal deficit
flashcards.clinical_why: Acute neuro change requires immediate escalation.

flashcards.front: Neurological — Strength 5/5 all extremities bilaterally. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.clinical_why: Unilateral weakness suggests stroke until ruled out.

flashcards.front: Neurological — Steady gait, no assistive device needed. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal: Unsteady gait, inability to move extremity
flashcards.clinical_why: Gait change may reflect weakness or orthostasis.

flashcards.front: Neurological — No seizure activity observed. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Protect airway, time seizure, notify provider

flashcards.front: Cardiovascular — Apical pulse regular 78/min, S1/S2 without extra sounds. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: ECG if symptomatic; continuous monitoring if ordered
flashcards.clinical_why: New arrhythmia may reduce cardiac output.

flashcards.front: Cardiovascular — No pitting edema; JVD not visible at 45°. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Daily weights, strict I&O, notify provider
flashcards.clinical_why: Edema + JVD suggest fluid overload/heart failure.

flashcards.front: Cardiovascular — BP 118/72, HR 72, warm pink extremities. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: IV fluids per order; notify provider

flashcards.front: Cardiovascular — Chest pain absent; denies SOB. Normal or abnormal?
Respiratory — Symmetric chest rise; clear breath sounds. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.clinical_why: Increased work of breathing signals distress.

flashcards.front: Respiratory — SpO₂ 97% on room air, RR 16. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Apply O₂, reassess, notify if not improving
flashcards.clinical_why: Hypoxemia needs correction before routine care.

flashcards.front: Respiratory — No cough; sputum clear/white if present. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal: Productive cough with colored sputum, hemoptysis

flashcards.front: Respiratory — No wheezes; good air entry all fields. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Bronchodilator per order; prepare for escalation
flashcards.clinical_why: Silent chest in asthma = impending failure.

flashcards.front: Gastrointestinal — Abdomen soft, non-tender; active BS ×4. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: NPO if surgical concern; notify provider

flashcards.front: Gastrointestinal — Regular BM pattern; no nausea. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Monitor H&H; GI bleed protocol if indicated
flashcards.clinical_why: GI bleeding can cause hemodynamic collapse.

flashcards.front: Gastrointestinal — No rebound tenderness or rigidity. Normal or abnormal?
Gastrointestinal — Last BM yesterday, soft brown stool. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.clinical_why: Coffee-ground emesis suggests upper GI bleed.

flashcards.front: Genitourinary — Voiding without difficulty; urine clear yellow. Normal or abnormal?
Genitourinary — No suprapubic fullness. Normal or abnormal?
Genitourinary — Urine output 50 mL/hr. Normal or abnormal?
Genitourinary — No flank pain or fever. Normal or abnormal?
Musculoskeletal — Full ROM all joints without pain. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.clinical_why: Pain-limited ROM after injury needs evaluation.

flashcards.front: Musculoskeletal — Strength 5/5 major muscle groups. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.clinical_why: New weakness may be neuro emergency.

flashcards.front: Musculoskeletal — Steady gait, independent ambulation. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal: Unable to bear weight after fall
flashcards.clinical_why: Hip fracture common in older adult falls.

flashcards.front: Musculoskeletal — No swelling or deformity at joints. Normal or abnormal?
Integumentary (Skin) — Skin warm, dry, intact; turgor elastic. Normal or abnormal?
Integumentary (Skin) — Even skin color; no open areas. Normal or abnormal?
Integumentary (Skin) — No pressure injury present. Normal or abnormal?
Integumentary (Skin) — Small bruise 1 cm, non-tender. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.clinical_why: Purpura + fever may indicate serious infection.

flashcards.front: HEENT — PERRLA; conjunctiva pink; sclera white. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.clinical_why: New anisocoria with LOC change = neuro emergency.

flashcards.front: HEENT — Moist pink oral mucosa. Normal or abnormal?
HEENT — Face symmetric at rest and with smile. Normal or abnormal?
HEENT — Nares patent bilaterally; no drainage. Normal or abnormal?
Psychosocial — Mood appropriate; denies SI/HI. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.clinical_why: Active SI with plan requires immediate safety.

flashcards.front: Psychosocial — Coherent thought process; engaged. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.clinical_why: Psychosis may impair participation in care.

flashcards.front: Psychosocial — Identifies support persons. Normal or abnormal?
Psychosocial — Calm, cooperative with care. Normal or abnormal?
Endocrine & Immune — Thyroid non-palpable; no tremor. Normal or abnormal?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Vitals; TSH per order; notify provider

flashcards.front: Endocrine & Immune — Lymph nodes small, mobile, non-tender. Normal or abnormal?
Endocrine & Immune — Skin intact; wounds healing normally. Normal or abnormal?
Endocrine & Immune — Fingerstick glucose 110 mg/dL, alert. Normal or abnormal?
RED FLAG — Sudden worst headache + stiff neck + fever. Priority action?
RED FLAG — Sudden unilateral leg swelling, warmth, pain. Priority action?
RED FLAG — Stridor at rest, SpO₂ 90%. Priority action?
RED FLAG — Rigid board-like abdomen with rebound. Priority action?
RED FLAG — Post-op anuric 10 hr, suprapubic distension. Priority action?
RED FLAG — Cast pain with passive toe extension, cool toes. Priority action?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Notify immediately; do not elevate leg.

flashcards.front: RED FLAG — Wound with exposed bone on coccyx. Priority action?
RED FLAG — Battle sign + clear fluid from nose post trauma. Priority action?
RED FLAG — Suicide plan with pills in room. Priority action?
RED FLAG — Glucose 42 mg/dL, diaphoresis, confusion. Priority action?
flashcards.abnormal_action: 15 g fast carb if able; recheck 15 min.

flashcards.front: RED FLAG — Asthma: silent chest, exhausted, SpO₂ 88%. Priority action?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Rapid response; continuous nebs per order.

flashcards.front: RED FLAG — HR 168 irregular, BP 86/50, dizzy. Priority action?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Monitor; notify provider; ACLS per protocol.

flashcards.front: RED FLAG — Absent BS, distension, feculent vomiting. Priority action?
flashcards.abnormal_action: NPO, NG per order, notify surgeon.

flashcards.front: RED FLAG — GCS 15→12 over 2 hr post head injury. Priority action?
flashcards.abnormal_action: Neuro checks q15min; CT per protocol.

flashcards.front: RED FLAG — Infant RR 65, nasal flaring, retractions, SpO₂ 91%. Priority action?
Temp 101.8°F, HR 112, BP 102/64. Normal or abnormal?
Orthostatics: lying 120/80 → standing 100/70 with dizziness.
flashcards.abnormal: Orthostatic hypotension ≥20 SBP drop with symptoms.

practice_questions.question: Which finding requires immediate notification?
options: SpO₂ 88% with RR 32 and accessory use
practice_questions.explanation: SpO₂ 88% with tachypnea and accessory muscle use signals respiratory compromise — assess airway, position for breathing, apply O₂ per protocol, and notify the provider immediately.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Hypoxemia with increased work of breathing is an ABC priority that can progress to respiratory failure within minutes.

practice_questions.question: Best technique order for abdominal assessment?
practice_questions.explanation: Inspect, auscultate, percuss, then palpate (IAPPA). Auscultate bowel sounds before palpation so palpation does not alter sound quality.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Altered bowel sounds from palpation can mask true peristaltic activity and delay recognition of ileus or obstruction.

practice_questions.question: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as:
options: SBP drop ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg with symptoms on standing
Any BP change lying to standing
practice_questions.explanation: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as a drop in SBP ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing, often with dizziness or syncope.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Orthostatic changes identify volume depletion, autonomic dysfunction, and fall risk — especially in older adults on antihypertensives.

practice_questions.question: COPD patient O₂ target SpO₂ is typically:
practice_questions.explanation: Chronic CO₂ retainers depend on hypoxic drive; target SpO₂ 88–92% to avoid suppressing ventilation and causing CO₂ narcosis.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Over-oxygenating COPD patients can precipitate respiratory acidosis, somnolence, and need for mechanical ventilation.

practice_questions.question: FAST assessment includes:
practice_questions.explanation: FAST (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call) is a community and bedside stroke screening tool — time last known well is critical for thrombolytic eligibility.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Every minute of untreated stroke loses ~1.9 million neurons; rapid recognition drives door-to-needle times.

practice_questions.question: During head-to-toe assessment, which sequence is correct?
options: Head-to-toe general survey, then focused systems
practice_questions.explanation: Begin with a general survey (appearance, ABCs), then proceed systematically head-to-toe or by priority when instability is present.
practice_questions.clinical_why: A structured sequence ensures no system is skipped and unstable findings are caught early.

practice_questions.question: A patient has unequal pupils with a fixed dilated right pupil after head trauma. Priority action?
options: Notify provider immediately; continue neuro checks
practice_questions.explanation: Unilateral fixed dilated pupil (blown pupil) after trauma suggests expanding intracranial pressure or herniation — escalate immediately.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Pupil changes may be the earliest sign of uncal herniation before altered LOC.

practice_questions.question: When auscultating heart sounds, the nurse should:
options: Use the diaphragm at four primary valve areas with patient supine and left lateral
Use bell only at the carotid
Skip auscultation if pulse is regular
practice_questions.explanation: Systematic auscultation at aortic, pulmonic, tricuspid, and mitral areas (and Erb's point) with appropriate patient positions improves murmur detection.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Murmurs and extra heart sounds are location-specific; incomplete auscultation misses valvular pathology.

practice_questions.question: Crackles heard on lung auscultation most often indicate:
options: Fluid in alveoli or small airways
practice_questions.explanation: Fine crackles (rales) suggest alveolar fluid — CHF, pneumonia, or atelectasis; assess O₂ saturation and work of breathing.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Crackles plus hypoxemia may signal pulmonary edema requiring diuretics and O₂ before cardiac arrest.

practice_questions.question: The best pain assessment approach for a nonverbal intubated ICU patient is:
options: Assume no pain if patient cannot speak
practice_questions.explanation: Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool (CPOT) and Behavioral Pain Scale use facial expression, body movement, and ventilator compliance.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Untreated pain increases sympathetic drive, delirium, and ventilator days in ICU patients.

practice_questions.question: Capillary refill >3 seconds in an adult with cool extremities suggests:
practice_questions.explanation: Delayed cap refill with cool skin indicates poor peripheral perfusion — shock, dehydration, or peripheral vascular disease.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Perfusion assessment complements vital signs when hypotension is not yet present.

practice_questions.question: Pitting edema rated 3+ means:
practice_questions.explanation: 3+ pitting is moderate — 4–6 mm depth; 4+ is severe (>6 mm, >30 sec rebound). Document location and bilateral comparison.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Quantified edema tracks fluid overload in heart failure, renal disease, and liver failure.

practice_questions.question: A Glasgow Coma Scale score of 8 indicates:
options: Severe brain injury — airway protection priority
practice_questions.explanation: GCS ≤8 is severe TBI — intubation may be needed to protect airway; trend GCS q1h or per protocol.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Declining GCS by ≥2 points warrants repeat imaging and neurosurgical consult.

practice_questions.question: When assessing jugular venous distension (JVD), the nurse should:
options: Elevate HOB 30–45° and measure vertical distance above sternal angle
practice_questions.explanation: JVD >3–4 cm above sternal angle at 45° suggests increased central venous pressure — right heart failure or fluid overload.
practice_questions.clinical_why: JVD distinguishes right-sided heart failure from isolated peripheral edema.

practice_questions.question: Bowel sounds absent for 5 minutes in a post-op abdomen may indicate:
options: Ileus or obstruction — correlate with nausea, distension, flatus
practice_questions.explanation: Absent sounds plus distension, pain, or no flatus warrant provider notification; post-op ileus is common but must be monitored.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Complete obstruction or paralytic ileus can cause bowel ischemia and perforation if unrecognized.

practice_questions.question: The correct technique for palpating the thyroid is:
options: Stand behind patient, displace trachea, palpate lobes during swallow
Palpate only from anterior without swallow
Auscultate thyroid for bruits first always
Skip if patient has no symptoms
practice_questions.explanation: Posterior approach with swallowing helps distinguish thyroid nodules from other neck masses.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Thyromegaly, nodules, and tenderness guide hyper/hypothyroid and thyroiditis workup.

practice_questions.question: A positive Homans sign (calf pain on dorsiflexion) is:
options: Not reliable alone for DVT — use Wells criteria and ultrasound
practice_questions.explanation: Homans sign has low sensitivity and specificity; assess unilateral swelling, warmth, Homan's only as one cue.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Missed DVT leads to pulmonary embolism — the leading preventable cause of hospital death.

practice_questions.question: When assessing skin turgor in an older adult, the nurse knows:
options: Turgor is less reliable — check mucous membranes and axillae
practice_questions.explanation: Aging reduces skin elasticity; assess tongue, mucosa, and recent weight/I&O for hydration in elders.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Dehydration in older adults causes confusion, orthostasis, and AKI.

practice_questions.question: Korotkoff phase V (disappearance of sound) is used for:
practice_questions.explanation: In adults, diastolic is the point sounds disappear (phase V); in children, phase IV (muffling) may be used per policy.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Consistent technique prevents false hypertension diagnosis and unnecessary treatment.

practice_questions.question: The most accurate core temperature route in critical care is:
practice_questions.explanation: Core temps reflect hypothalamic set point; pulmonary artery is gold standard; bladder acceptable with good urine flow.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Fever in neutropenia or post-op sepsis requires accurate trending for antibiotic timing.

practice_questions.question: During abdominal palpation, the nurse should:
options: Start light palpation away from painful quadrant, then deep if tolerated
Use one finger maximum pressure always
practice_questions.explanation: Light palpation first; guard against pain by starting away from tenderness; note rebound and rigidity as peritoneal signs.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Rebound tenderness and rigidity suggest surgical abdomen — NPO and surgical consult.

practice_questions.question: A patient with new unilateral leg swelling and erythema — priority assessment includes:
options: Measure calf circumference, pulse, cap refill, and pain
practice_questions.explanation: Unilateral swelling suggests DVT, cellulitis, or compartment syndrome — compare limbs and check pulses.
practice_questions.clinical_why: DVT prophylaxis failure and PE risk require prompt diagnostic imaging.

practice_questions.question: When using the ophthalmoscope, the nurse assesses:
options: Red reflex, optic disc, vessels, and macula
practice_questions.explanation: Fundoscopic exam detects papilledema, hemorrhages, and diabetic retinopathy changes.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Papilledema indicates increased ICP — pair with headache and neuro exam.

practice_questions.question: The Weber test lateralizes sound to the deaf ear in:
practice_questions.explanation: Weber: conductive loss lateralizes to affected ear; sensorineural lateralizes to better ear.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Differentiating conductive vs sensorineural loss guides cerumen impaction vs acoustic neuroma workup.

practice_questions.question: A respiratory rate of 28 with shallow breathing and use of accessory muscles indicates:
options: Increased work of breathing — assess SpO₂ and airway
practice_questions.explanation: Tachypnea with accessory use signals respiratory distress — position upright, assess patency, apply O₂, prepare for escalation.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Fatigue from increased work leads to CO₂ retention and respiratory arrest.

practice_questions.question: When assessing an older adult's cognition, the nurse should:
options: Use validated screen (Mini-Cog, MMSE) and compare baseline
practice_questions.explanation: Acute confusion differs from dementia — screen for delirium triggers: infection, meds, hypoxia, pain, constipation.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Delirium increases falls, length of stay, and mortality — treat cause, not only symptom.

practice_questions.question: Pediatric apical pulse is best counted:
options: At apex with stethoscope for full 60 seconds (1 min)
practice_questions.explanation: Apical count for full minute in infants/children — radial is inaccurate with tachycardia and arrhythmias.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Undetected tachycardia in dehydration or fever can precede shock in infants.

practice_questions.question: A Braden score of 14 indicates:
options: Moderate pressure injury risk — implement turning schedule
practice_questions.explanation: Braden ≤18 is at risk; 15–18 mild, 13–14 moderate, 10–12 high, ≤9 very high — initiate prevention bundle.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Hospital-acquired pressure injuries are largely preventable with repositioning and moisture management.

practice_questions.question: During breast examination, the nurse teaches:
options: Monthly self-exam after menses; report new lumps, discharge, skin changes
practice_questions.explanation: Clinical breast exam complements mammography; teach BSE timing and what changes to report.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Early breast cancer detection improves survival — patient education extends assessment beyond the hospital.

practice_questions.question: When documenting assessment findings, the nurse should:
options: Record objective data separately from subjective quotes
Use vague terms like 'good' without specifics
practice_questions.explanation: Objective (measurable) vs subjective (patient report) supports legal defensibility and care continuity.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Accurate documentation is communication — vague notes delay treatment and increase liability.

practice_questions.question: A patient reports chest pain 8/10. Before giving nitroglycerin, the nurse assesses:
options: Blood pressure and recent PDE-5 inhibitor use
practice_questions.explanation: Nitroglycerin contraindicated if SBP <90 or sildenafil/tadalafil within 24–48 hr — can cause severe hypotension.
practice_questions.clinical_why: ACS assessment includes 12-lead ECG, aspirin per protocol, and serial troponins — not pain med alone.

practice_questions.question: The nurse hears a systolic murmur loudest at the apex radiating to the axilla. This suggests:
practice_questions.explanation: Mitral regurgitation: holosystolic murmur at apex → axilla; assess for CHF symptoms and history of rheumatic fever.
practice_questions.clinical_why: New murmur with fever may indicate endocarditis — blood cultures before antibiotics.

practice_questions.question: When assessing peripheral pulses, absent dorsalis pedis bilaterally with cool feet requires:
options: Compare to baseline, check cap refill, notify if acute change
practice_questions.explanation: Absent DP pulses may be chronic (PVD) or acute (embolus) — compare sides, skin color, temperature, pain.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Acute limb ischemia (6 P's) is a surgical emergency within 6 hours.

practice_questions.question: A patient with COPD has barrel chest and prolonged expiration. The nurse expects:
options: Decreased breath sounds and pursed-lip breathing
practice_questions.explanation: COPD hyperinflation causes barrel chest, wheezes, diminished air movement, and pursed-lip breathing to prolong expiratory phase.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Baseline differs from acute exacerbation — sudden change in sputum or SpO₂ needs treatment escalation.

practice_questions.question: During mental status exam, the nurse assesses orientation by asking:
practice_questions.explanation: Orientation ×4 is standard; also assess attention, memory, mood, and thought content.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Acute disorientation suggests delirium, stroke, hypoglycemia, or hypoxia — not always dementia.

practice_questions.question: The nurse notes clubbing of fingers. This is associated with:
practice_questions.explanation: Digital clubbing (increased nail angle) develops over months with chronic hypoxia or inflammatory conditions.
practice_questions.clinical_why: New clubbing warrants chest imaging — may be first sign of lung malignancy.

practice_questions.question: When measuring a wound, the nurse documents:
options: Length × width × depth, tunneling, odor, drainage type
practice_questions.explanation: Standard wound measurement in cm; note undermining, sinus tracts, periwound skin, and infection signs.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Trending wound size guides dressing selection and surgical vs conservative management.

practice_questions.question: A pregnant patient at 28 weeks — appropriate fundal height expectation is:
options: Approximately 28 cm (±2–3 cm) from symphysis to fundus
practice_questions.explanation: After 20 weeks, fundal height in cm roughly equals gestational age — discrepancy suggests IUGR, twins, or polyhydramnios.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Fundal height discordance triggers ultrasound for fetal growth assessment.

practice_questions.question: The nurse performing a focused musculoskeletal exam after fall assesses:
options: Range of motion, strength, alignment, and neurovascular status distal to injury
practice_questions.explanation: Compare bilateral limbs; check pulses, sensation, movement before and after splinting.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Compartment syndrome and neurovascular compromise are time-sensitive complications of fractures.

practice_questions.question: Which finding during shift handoff requires the receiving nurse to assess first?
options: New oxygen requirement with SpO₂ 90% on room air
practice_questions.explanation: Handoff prioritization follows ABCs — new hypoxemia indicates potential deterioration requiring immediate assessment.
practice_questions.clinical_why: SBAR handoff must highlight unstable trends so the oncoming nurse can intervene before decompensation.
